personality_voice.book_baseline: In the books, Roger Davies is a recurring minor character with a consistent and lightly comic personality: he is socially confident enough to captain the Ravenclaw Quidditch team and ask out Fleur Delacour, yet he is visibly overwhelmed by her beauty - missing his mouth with his fork, parroting her opinions without processing them ('Absolutely right,' slapping the table in imitation), and speaking in an 'oddly husky voice' while trying to impress her. He is also seen later in a comfortable relationship with a blonde girl at Madam Puddifoot's, kissing her openly, which provides a mild comic contrast to Harry's awkward date with Cho. His voice is brief but characterised: eager, slightly dazed, socially performative.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The film corpus is entirely empty. Roger Davies does not appear in any provided screenplay scene.
personality_voice.difference: Roger Davies is entirely absent from the film corpus. No dialogue, action, reaction, or presence of any kind is recorded.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: A score of 0 across all dimensions is the only defensible result when a character has no film presence whatsoever. The scoring framework cannot award points for faithfulness that cannot be evaluated. This is not a penalty for compression - it is the absence of any film material to score.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Roger Davies serves several small but distinct narrative functions: he is the Ravenclaw Quidditch captain (opponent to Wood in book 3), Fleur Delacour's Yule Ball date (providing comic relief through his dazed infatuation), a background presence in the Snape/Dumbledore conversation scene (his and Fleur's giggling return from the grounds prompts Dumbledore's question about Karkaroff), and a presence at Madam Puddifoot's that creates social pressure on Harry during his date with Cho. He also appears as a name on the Quidditch roster in book 5. His agency is modest but real: he asks Cho out (she mentions this), he escorts Fleur, he is present in scenes that move other characters' emotional states.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: Absent from the film corpus entirely.
narrative_role_agency.difference: All narrative functions are absent or presumably transferred/dropped.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: No film presence means no agency can be evaluated or credited.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Roger's motivations are simple but legible: social ambition and attraction. He pursues Fleur as a date, is visibly motivated by her beauty to the point of losing composure, and later appears in a stable romantic pairing. There is no internal conflict of depth, but his motivation (romantic/social pursuit, desire to impress) is consistent and observable.

character_arc.book_baseline: Roger Davies has no meaningful arc - he is a flat supporting character whose function is contextual and comic. His arc, such as it is, moves from Quidditch captain to Yule Ball date (dazed by Fleur) to comfortable relationship at Madam Puddifoot's. This is intentionally flat and incidental.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Roger's most defined relationship is with Fleur Delacour at the Yule Ball - a comic dynamic in which he is entirely overwhelmed and she is entirely indifferent to his attention, focused on criticising Hogwarts. His later relationship with the unnamed blonde girl at Madam Puddifoot's is a minor but functional contrast to Harry and Cho. His mention by Cho ('He asked me out... I turned him down') also places him in a minor relational triangle with Harry and Cho.

comparative.key_observations: Roger Davies has no presence in the provided film corpus. Every score is 0 not as a faithfulness judgment but as a reflection of the impossibility of evaluation: there is no film portrayal to compare against the book baseline. Confidence is Low across all dimensions for the same reason. The lost material is real - Roger Davies performs several small but distinct narrative and comic functions in the books (Yule Ball infatuation comedy, Quidditch captain, background presence in key scenes, social pressure catalyst at Madam Puddifoot's) - but whether these were adapted, transferred, or simply cut cannot be determined from the corpus alone. A meaningful FP score for Roger Davies would require film scenes in which he appears.
